title: practical english usage - Part 183
content:
Who(m)  and which can also be the objects of verbs in relative clauses.  Whom  is This is the back door key, which also opens the garage.

I like people who smile a lot.

Who and which can be the subjects of verbs in relative clauses.

2 subject      and   object not It's a book which will interest children of all ages, (     ... a book who ...) man-whkh ...) not What's the name of the tall man who just came in? (     .. .-the-tall The  relative pronouns who and whom refer to people; which refers to things.

1  who,     whom       and   which 233 relatives:     basic    information grammar •  234   identifying and non-identifying clauses 'non-identifying', 'non-defining' or 'non-restrictive' relative clauses.) a person or thing that is already identified. (In grammars, these are called Other relative clauses do not identify or classify; they simply tell us more about Have you got something that will get paint out of a carpet?

Who owns the car which is parked outside?

People who take physical exercise live longer.

What's the name of the tall man who just came in?

'identifying', 'defining' or 'restrictive' relative clauses.) thing, or which  kind of person or thing, is meant. (In grammars, these are called Some relative clauses identify or classify nouns: they tell us which  person or 1  two    kinds    of  relative      clause the    tall    man     who     .. Mr   Rogers,       who     ...

234 identifying        and   non-identifying          clauses: (= ... the reason for which  ...) Do you know the reason why she doesn't like me?

Why is used in a similar way after reason.

Do you know a shop where I can find sandals? (= ... a shop at which  ...) I'll never forget the day when I first met you. (= ... the day on which  ...) place. They  are used in the same way as preposition + which.

When and where can introduce relative clauses after nouns referring to time and 9 relative      when,      where       and   why (NOT   She cycled... what is pretty good...) She cycled from London to Glasgow, which is pretty good for a woman of 75.

(     ..., what surprised everybody.) He got married again a year later, which surprised everybody.

Note that what cannot be used in this way.

Which can refer not only to a noun, but also to the whole of a previous clause.

8  which       referring       to  a  whole     clause came down ...) not I saw a girl whose hair came down to her waist. (     ... whose her hair It replaces his/her/its. For more details, ►    235.

Whose is a possessive relative pronoun, used as a determiner before nouns.

7 whose:    a  girl    whose       hair    .. .

I've found the keys that you were asking about. (     .. .that you were asking not Are there any papers that I can look at? (     .. .that I can look at them?) Prepositional verbs, like others, only have one object.

Here's an article which might interest you. (     .. .which it might - Here's an article. It might interest you.

This is Mr Rogers, whom you met last year. (     ... whom you met him - This is Mr Rogers, You met him  last year.

Relative ClausesSection     21 grammar •  234   identifying and non-identifying clauses For sentences like It was lack who told the police (‘ cleft sentences*), ►    273.

a glass of wine, he drank at once.) not I poured him  a glass of wine, which he drank at once, (     I poured him - Did you like the wine we drank last night?

She met my brother, whom she later married. (OT   She met my  brother, she - I feel sorry for the man she married.

especially in an informal style. In non-identifying clauses this is not possible.

In identifying relative clauses, we  often leave out pronouns if they are objects, 4 leaving      out   object     pronouns not This is Naomi, who sells the tickets, (     This is Naomi, that sells the tickets.) - Where’s the girl who/that sells the tickets?

(     .. . 'The Old Man and the Sea', that is really easy to read.) I lent him  ’ The Old Man and the Sea’, which is really easy to read.

- Have you got a book which/that is really easy to read?
